perturbation.original_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form; marking as 'Confidential' is optional.
perturbation.explanation: By making the 'Confidential' marking optional, the contract creates ambiguity about what constitutes confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets partly by reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Removing the mandatory marking of 'Confidential' undermines such reasonable efforts, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The changed text makes marking confidential information as 'Confidential' optional, which contradicts the requirement of 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' as described in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under the UTSA. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, the UTSA's intention, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, is to resolve inconsistencies in trade secret protection across states, implying a standard of care is required. By making confidentiality markings optional, the contract weakens the reasonable efforts needed to protect trade secrets, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information shall be disclosed to only those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a need to know the information 2 <PAGE> and who are obligated under terms no less restrictive than those imposed by this Agreement on the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information may be disclosed to people within its organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a potential interest in the information 2 <PAGE> and who are informed of the confidential nature of the information.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'need to know' to 'potential interest' and 'obligated under terms no less restrictive' to 'informed of the confidential nature' broadens the scope of permissible disclosure and weakens the obligation to protect the information, potentially conflicting with export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR controls the release of certain technologies and information to foreign nationals, even within the US. Loosening the restrictions on disclosure increases the risk of violating export control regulations if the 'Confidential Information' includes controlled technology.

perturbation.justification: The original text restricts disclosure to those with a 'need to know' who are 'obligated under terms no less restrictive,' aligning with the goal of controlled information dissemination, as suggested by the Law Explanation mentioning export control regulations. The changed text broadens this to anyone with a 'potential interest' who is merely 'informed of the confidential nature,' creating a much weaker obligation. The EAR, as indicated in 'Scraped Law Snippet 2,' controls the export of certain technologies, and loosening restrictions increases the risk of violating these regulations if the 'Confidential Information' includes controlled technology. The change significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with export control laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Japan.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the jurisdiction where Hybrid Networks, Inc. is headquartered.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity and potential conflict regarding governing law. While the original text specifies Japanese law, the modified text depends on where Hybrid Networks, Inc. is headquartered at any given time. If the company moves its headquarters, the governing law changes. Because the contract explicitly stated which location it governs, now it would be interpreted by where the company is physically located. If this is in a different country or state, there may be multiple legal interpretations, which is not allowed in most states.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws addresses choice of law in contract disputes. While parties can generally choose the governing law, the chosen law must have a reasonable relationship to the parties or the transaction and not violate a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. The modification introduces uncertainty and may not be enforceable if the location of headquarters changes and bears no reasonable relationship to the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by creating ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the governing law. The original text clearly specifies Japanese law. The changed text introduces a variable factor - the location of Hybrid Networks, Inc.'s headquarters, which could change over time, making the governing law uncertain. This uncertainty contradicts the principle of clear choice of law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' The modification introduces this conflict as the applicable law now depends on a variable factor, leading to potential disputes over which jurisdiction's law applies.

perturbation.original_text: The Disclosing Party does not make any representation or warranty, except as may be specifically provided in writing, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, or as to its utility or suitability for any purpose of the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party expressly disclaims any right of the Receiving Party to rely thereon, or any liability to the Receiving Party resulting from the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party does not make any representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information unless fraudulent misrepresentation can be proven, or as to its utility or suitability for any purpose of the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party expressly disclaims any right of the Receiving Party to rely thereon, or any liability to the Receiving Party resulting from the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By adding "unless fraudulent misrepresentation can be proven", the clause implies that the disclosing party can be liable for inaccurate or incomplete information if fraudulent misrepresentation is demonstrated, which contradicts the blanket disclaimer of liability. This creates a contradiction, by both disclaiming and acknowledging liability under specific circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 restricts contracts that exempt responsibility for fraud or willful injury. While the original clause disclaims any liability for the Confidential Information's accuracy, the modification implicitly allows for liability if fraudulent misrepresentation is proven. This creates a conflict and may render the disclaimer unenforceable under California law, as the contract now suggests liability for fraudulent misrepresentation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces an exception to the disclaimer of liability, specifically for fraudulent misrepresentation. California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts which aim to exempt someone from responsibility for their own fraud are against public policy. By initially disclaiming all liability and then adding an exception for fraud, the contract creates a contradiction, suggesting that liability could exist for fraudulent misrepresentation, which is exactly what the original clause intended to prevent as per CIV 1668. Therefore, the added clause clashes with the purpose of the original disclaimer and the statute.
